rubric_en: The heartbreak of hoping for a democratic Iran
rubric_zh: 对民主伊朗的希望所带来的心碎

paragraphs.en: Tehran, 2006 (Abbas / Magnum)
paragraphs.zh: 德黑兰，2006（Abbas / Magnum）

paragraphs.en: This article was featured in the One Story to Read Today newsletter. Sign up for it here.
paragraphs.zh: 本文曾刊登在 One Story to Read Today 新闻简报中。欢迎在这里订阅。

paragraphs.en: Like Donald Trump, I, too, once underestimated the Islamic Republic of Iran. In the fall of 2004, as an underemployed freelance journalist drawn to heady stories about international politics, I had the bright idea of traveling to the notoriously closed country on a tourist visa. Press visas for Iran were hard to come by, and my travel was exploratory—I had no particular assignment. My profile was low, I figured. Who would care if, between the obligatory sightseeing expeditions, I rattled around Iranian cities meeting political analysts, philosophers, students, filmmakers, and the relatives of Iranian expats I knew?
paragraphs.zh: 就像唐纳德·特朗普一样，我也曾低估过伊斯兰共和国。2004 年秋天，我还是一名业余接单的自由记者，刚被国际政治里的那些高风险故事吸引，就有了一个大胆的想法——持旅游签证前往这个以“封闭”著称的国家。当时去伊朗拿记者签证很难，而我的行程也只是一次探索，没有任何具体采访任务。我想自己的知名度很低。谁会在我顺着必去的观光线路穿梭于伊朗各城时，在我与政治分析人士、哲学家、学生、电影人以及我认识的一些伊朗侨民家属见面时在意呢？

paragraphs.en: The Islamic Republic was not to be messed with in this way. Its visa regime was deadly serious; so was the official paranoia about foreigners. American tourists were required to travel with a specially vetted guide. For four weeks, I strained to see past the diminutive figure of a young woman I’ll call Pardis, who pretended to be a tour guide while I pretended to be a tourist. Pardis excelled at her job, which was not only to make sure that I adhered to the terms of my visa, but also to report on all of my movements and conversations, and to obfuscate everything I saw.
paragraphs.zh: 伊斯兰共和国不会允许这种方式来对待它。它的签证制度非常严肃，官方对外国人的戒备也同样严重。美国游客被要求必须由经过特别审查的导游陪同。四个星期里，我努力在一位我姑且称作帕尔迪斯的年轻女子身影后面看清现实。她假装自己是导游，而我则假装自己是游客。帕尔迪斯在工作上做得很好：她不仅要确保我遵守签证规定，还要报告我所有的行踪和谈话，并对我所看到的一切进行遮掩。

paragraphs.en: One day I watched a bus disgorge a troop of uniformed Basij militiamen at an intersection in central Tehran.
paragraphs.zh: 有一天，我在德黑兰市中心的一个十字路口看见一辆巴士把一队穿着制服的巴斯伊杰民兵放了下来。

paragraphs.en: “Who are they?” I asked Pardis.
paragraphs.zh: “他们是谁？”我问帕尔迪斯。

paragraphs.en: “Oh,” she said. “They’re a youth group. Sometimes they help the police.”
paragraphs.zh: “哦，”她说，“他们是一个青年组织。有时他们会帮警察。”

paragraphs.en: Because Pardis stood between me and all that I was truly curious about, I studied her. She was not a dour Islamist but a fun-loving 31-year-old who had hair flowing out of her headscarf and risqué online flirtations with men overseas. She was an orphan, unlucky in love, and ambitious in her minder-ing, circumstances that rendered her marginal—an unmarried career woman living with a roommate. She was also relentlessly trivial, with a knack for diverting any potentially substantive encounter I might have with her country or anyone in it into an endless stream of repetitive inside jokes and girlish banter.
paragraphs.zh: 因为帕尔迪斯把我和我真正好奇的一切都隔开了，我只能观察她。她并不是一个阴郁的伊斯兰主义者，而是一个爱玩笑的31岁女性，头巾外面还留着飘出的头发，并且会和海外的男性进行有些大胆的网聊。她是孤儿，在感情上不太走运，也很有进取心，这些处境让她显得很边缘——一个未婚、独立生活并和室友同住的职业女性。她也总是带着一种轻佻的气质，总能把我本可能与她这个国家或这个国家里任何人进行的任何有分量的交流，转向无穷无尽、重复的内部玩笑和少女般的调侃。

paragraphs.en: Read: The ‘existential anxiety’ of the Islamic Republic
paragraphs.zh: 阅读：伊斯兰共和国的“生存焦虑”

paragraphs.en: We wandered through bazaars, threatening to buy each other the ugliest items we saw—a giant pair of red satin underwear, a wig, a dowdy zebra-print skirt. We flew to Shiraz on IranAir, a black-turbaned cleric across the aisle from us. Pardis took out a bottle of polish, began painting her nails, and smiled at me impishly. “In front of the mullah!” she said in her little voice. (He was absorbed in opening his airline-issue carton of apple juice.)
paragraphs.zh: 我们在集市里闲逛，互相威胁要给对方买下我们看到的最难看东西——一双巨大的红色缎面内裤、一顶假发、一条土气的斑马纹裙。我们搭乘伊朗航空飞往设拉子，机舱过道对面坐着一位戴着黑色头巾的神职人员。帕迪斯拿出一瓶指甲油开始涂指甲，调皮地对我一笑，说道：“在这位毛拉维（伊斯兰教士）面前！”（他正专心打开航空公司发的苹果汁纸盒。）

paragraphs.en: Pardis was not invested in anything that the Islamic Republic seemed to care about. But she was, for professional reasons, invested in exercising control over me. For my safety, she insisted, I could never be without her protective presence. But when she entered a room—even, memorably, one where I sat talking with members of her own family about their feelings about the hijab—everyone stopped talking.
paragraphs.zh: 帕迪斯并不关心伊斯兰共和国似乎关心的一切事情。可出于职业上的原因，她却非常在意对我进行控制。她坚持说为了安全起见，我不能离开她的保护。可一旦她进了房间——包括一次让我印象深刻的时候，她坐在我旁边和她自己家人的成员谈论他们对头巾的看法——屋里的人就都停了下来。

paragraphs.en: Privately, she’d tell me about her love interests. Relationships between unmarried men and women were commonplace but forbidden under the Islamic Republic. Suddenly she’d freeze in fear and implore me not to tell anyone, or backtrack and claim that she was talking about a friend. Toward the end of our month together, in the shadow of a breakup, she sat smoking and brooding in my hotel room. I told her that Iranian women seemed forced to live complicated lives.
paragraphs.zh: 她会私下告诉我自己的恋爱对象。未婚男女的关系在这里司空见惯，却在伊斯兰共和国被明令禁止。忽然间，她会被恐惧定住，恳求我不要告诉任何人，或是立刻打退堂鼓，改口说自己只是聊到一个朋友。临近我们共同相处一个月的结束，在分手阴影下，她坐在我酒店房间里抽烟发呆。我告诉她，伊朗女性似乎被迫过着复杂的生活。

paragraphs.en: She replied with uncharacteristic bluntness: “Better to say that women here find ways to kill lots of things inside themselves.”
paragraphs.zh: 她用一种少见的坦率回应道：“与其说她们能做很多事，不如说这里的女性会想办法把自己心里许多东西先杀掉。”

paragraphs.en: Pardis was not interested in politics, but I was. What had drawn me to Iran was a political and philosophical movement that seemed unique in the Muslim world. A circle of the most radical revolutionary elites—hostage takers, religious philosophers, former officials, even founders of the security forces—had fallen out of political favor in the early 1990s and spent the better part of a decade remaking themselves as proponents of incremental democratic reform. They produced an entire theoretical literature that drew on Western and Islamic sources; they mobilized young people to support their campaigns for elected office; and they tried to clean up abuses in some parts of the government they ran. The reformists were insiders who intended not to destroy the regime, but to liberalize it. They sought to make the supreme leader a benign figurehead—like the Queen of England, they sometimes said. The supreme leader had other plans.
paragraphs.zh: 我对政治很感兴趣，她却不感兴趣。吸引我去伊朗的是一场在穆斯林世界里似乎独特的政治与思想运动。一群最激进的革命精英——包括劫机者、宗教哲学家、前官员，甚至安全部门的创始人——在1990年代初失势，之后整整近十年几乎全部时间都在重塑自己，转型为渐进式民主改革的倡导者。他们写成了一整套理论著作，吸纳了西方与伊斯兰的思想源流；他们动员年轻人支持自己参与选举的竞选；并且试图清理他们掌管的一些政府部门中的弊端。改革派是体制内的人，他们的目标不是推翻政权，而是把政权温和化。曾有人说过，他们想把最高领袖做成一个象征性的温和角色，像英国女王那样。可最高领袖另有打算。

paragraphs.en: My first visit to the country was comically unsuited to exploring any of this. By day, Pardis was obligated to fully occupy my time. Some of what we saw was splendid: palaces and gardens; museums of carpets, miniatures, and Islamic calligraphy; even madrassas and shrines. Then Ramadan set in, and all museums closed. Pardis had us driven around in circles or held me all but captive in her apartment, watching music videos on satellite television. After she dropped me at my hotel in the evenings, I went out to meetings I’d arranged on my own. She was livid when she learned of this. I needed to bring her with me, she insisted, or she’d lose her job. She threatened to sit in my hotel lobby until midnight to make sure I didn’t leave—unless, she said, I agreed to give her the names of everyone I saw.
paragraphs.zh: 我对这段历史的第一次实地访问，讽刺得几乎失去了探索意义。白天，帕迪丝需要我把时间全部交给她。我们看到的一些东西当然很美：宫殿和花园；地毯、微缩画和伊斯兰书法博物馆；甚至还有经学院和圣陵。随后斋月到来，所有博物馆都关闭了。帕迪丝便让我在车里兜圈子，或者几乎把我囚在她的公寓里，让我看卫星电视上的音乐视频。每天晚上她把我送回酒店后，我又按自己安排去参加会面。她后来知道这件事后非常生气。她坚持说我该带她一起去，否则她会丢掉工作。她威胁说，除非我同意把见到的每个人姓名都告诉她，否则她会一直坐在我酒店前厅待到午夜，确保我不走。

paragraphs.en: Sure, I said. I’d give her all of the names before I left. I never intended to do this, and she never again asked me to. Maybe whoever needed to know about my movements already did. Or maybe Pardis covered for me—because she was lonely and considered me a friend, or because she feared she had told me too many of her secrets. Possibly she was simply satisfied that she had already done her job. I returned to New York in relative darkness about the reform movement and loath to write about the one thing I really knew, which was Pardis, and the story of how an otherwise indifferent person comes to hold a stake in a brutal regime—how she forces that stake on others just as it was forced on her.
paragraphs.zh: 当然可以，行。我告诉她，在离开前我会把所有人的名字都告诉她。我本来就不打算那样做，她之后也再没提起过。我想，或许早有人在背后掌握我的行踪；或者，帕迪丝是在替我遮掩——要么因为她的孤独，把我当成了朋友，要么因为她担心自己向我透露了太多秘密。也可能只是她觉得自己已经把该做的工作都做完了。回到纽约后，我关于改革运动的理解仍然模糊，也不愿写我真正了解的那件事：帕迪丝本人，以及一个原本冷漠的人如何与一个残暴政权发生牵连——她如何把这种牵连强加在自己身上，也强加在别人身上，就像这种牵连曾经被强加到她身上一样。

paragraphs.en: During the reformist presidency of Mohammad Khatami, from 1997 to 2005, a window opened wide enough for a democratic-minded civil society to draw breath in Iran. A crop of semi-independent newspapers sprouted, along with investigative journalists who dared to write for them. Cultural and philosophical magazines published searching essays on religion and the state. Young people formed NGOs to address an array of civic needs; some ran for newly formed city and provincial councils. Student activism spilled onto the streets.
paragraphs.zh: 在穆罕默德·哈塔米担任改革派总统期间（1997年至2005年），伊朗出现了一道足够宽的窗口，让怀抱民主理念的公民社会得以喘息。一批半独立报刊应运而生，配合敢于为其写稿的调查记者。文化与哲学杂志也开始刊登关于宗教与国家关系的深入文章。年轻人建立了非政府组织来回应各种公共需求；一些人还参与了新成立的市政和省级议会选举。学生运动进一步走上街头。

paragraphs.en: Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei did his best to slam this window shut. His henchmen tortured journalists and student activists in prison until they made humiliating confessions on national television. State-linked thugs beat up a philosopher at his lectures and shot a political theorist point-blank on the steps of Tehran’s city hall. Even so, a real infrastructure for democratic change persisted for a time—in the form of people who had the training and experience to run newspapers and civic organizations, citizens who expected these things to be allowed, and the semblance of a political network that connected society to the ministries of the state.
paragraphs.zh: 最高领袖阿里·哈梅内伊尽最大努力把这扇窗尽快关上。他的走狗在监狱里折磨记者和学生活动人士，逼迫他们在全国电视上作出屈辱的“认罪”表态。与政权相关的暴力分子在一位哲学家授课时殴打他，并在德黑兰市政厅台阶前当场枪杀一位政治理论家。即便如此，真正服务于民主变革的基础设施仍在一段时间里得以延续：既有受过训练并有经验的人在经营报社和公民组织，也有期望这些事务得以合法运作的公民，以及一种将社会与国家各部委联系起来的政治网络雏形。

paragraphs.en: Two presidential elections tested the resilience of this infrastructure. I covered the first of these, in 2005, with a proper journalist’s visa and a minder in her mid-40s who had a deep smoker’s rasp and a loud, insistent warmth. Bahar (also not her real name—for their safety, I’m using pseudonyms for the private citizens I met) belonged to a lost generation of bohemian Boomers whose class and secular social milieu had been violently displaced by the 1979 revolution. Women who had once lived and studied abroad now gathered in homes that smelled of opium smoke, where husbands were absent or idle and grown children seemed adrift. I learned only later of money troubles, past prison sentences, and ethnic- or religious-minority status that must have contributed to the sense of profound isolation in those homes, where it mingled with something louche and lively and almost careless.
paragraphs.zh: 两次总统选举检验了这套基础设施的韧性。我报道了其中第一次（2005年），当时我持有正规的记者签证，并由一位40岁出头的看护陪同，她嗓音因长期吸烟而沙哑，性格热切而执着。巴哈尔（也不是她的真名——为了安全起见，我给我遇到的普通公民都用了化名）属于一代“迷失”的波普一代波西米亚派，她们的阶层与世俗社交环境在1979年革命中遭到剧烈颠覆。曾经在国外生活或求学的女性，如今会聚在弥漫着鸦片烟味的客厅里：丈夫要么缺席，要么无所事事，成年子女也似乎在各自的生活里飘忽无归。后来我才知道，这些家庭中的经济困境、既往监禁经历，以及可能来自少数民族或少数宗教身份的处境，必定都加重了这种深度孤立感；而这种孤立又与一种有些放浪、也鲜活近乎漫不经心的气质交织在一起。

paragraphs.en: Of all the handlers I was assigned to in Iran—I returned in 2006, 2008, and 2012—Bahar was the least beholden to the agency she worked for. I gave her a list of the people I intended to speak with, many of them reformist politicians, student activists, journalists, and former political prisoners. Her boss told her we’d wind up dead, like a photojournalist who had reported near Evin prison a few years before. Bahar was undeterred. The people on my list were heroes to her for standing up to the Islamic Republic, and she would not forfeit the opportunity to meet them. She told her own handlers that my modesty required us to hire a female driver, which is how we managed to get her best friend, Niki, to ferry us around in her red Peugeot.
paragraphs.zh: 在我所有在伊朗分配到的联络人中——我分别在2006年、2008年和2012年回国时都遇到过——巴哈尔是对自己所在机构最不依附的那位。我向她提供了计划采访名单，名单里有许多改革派政客、学生活动家、记者和前政治犯。她的上级告诉她，我们很可能会死去，就像几年之前一位在伊文监狱附近采访的照片记者一样。巴哈尔没有退缩。她眼中，我名单上的人都是对抗伊斯兰共和国的英雄，她不愿放弃见到他们的机会。她向自己的联络人解释，我的端庄和礼貌要求我们雇用一位女性司机，因此我们才得以让她最好的朋友妮基开她那辆红色标致车载我们四处奔走。

paragraphs.en: How to fully convey the eccentricity of my little entourage? Niki had the stark, exaggerated beauty of a fashion model, though she was gaunt and faded, with a thousand-yard stare. She was also mostly bald. She’d first shaved her head in 1979 to taunt the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps. By her telling, she went out bareheaded to test the hijab law, which required women to cover their hair.
paragraphs.zh: 怎么才能把我那支小小随行团的怪异气质完整说清楚呢？妮基有着极为鲜明、夸张的模特般美貌，尽管她消瘦、憔悴，眼神又空洞得像望向千码之外。她几乎是秃头的。她说自己早在1979年就把头发剃光，专门来嘲讽伊斯兰革命卫队。按她的说法，她是赤裸着头出门去试探头巾规定——那条规定要求女性必须把头发遮住。

paragraphs.en: Where is your scarf? a Guardsman asked her.
paragraphs.zh: “你的头巾呢？”一个卫队军官问她。

paragraphs.en: I don’t need one. I have no hair, she said.
paragraphs.zh: “我不需要。”她说，“我又没头发。”

paragraphs.en: Ah, he replied. But you are still a woman.
paragraphs.zh: 他回了一句：“可你毕竟是个女人。”

paragraphs.en: Now Niki swathed herself in layers of flowing garments that resembled ordinary hijab less than they suggested dervish, flower child, and grim reaper all at once. She, too, wanted to meet the people on my list, and at times she entered the room with us. Bringing Pardis to any meeting had cast a pall of annoyance mixed with fear. Bringing Bahar and Niki added an antic element. They were extravagantly maternal, often starstruck, and prone to tears. One incident remains particularly salient in my memory.
paragraphs.zh: 后来，尼基把自己包裹在一层层垂坠的衣袍里，这些衣袍看起来更像苦行僧、花儿少女和死神的混合体，而不像普通的头巾。她同样想去见我名单上的人，有时也会跟我们一起进屋。无论带着帕迪斯去参加任何会面，都会带来一股混合着烦恼和恐惧的阴影；而再带上巴哈尔和尼基，则更添了一种古怪的元素。她们过分地有母性的张力，常常像见到偶像一样目瞪口呆，而且很容易哭起来。我记得尤其清楚的一幕就发生在那时。

paragraphs.en: The reformists had flubbed the election I’d come to witness. They ran three candidates, and many liberal-minded Iranians rejected all of them, on the grounds that the reformist project was a failure and Iranian elections were far from free. And so the populist hard-liner Mahmoud Ahmadinejad—Khamenei’s favorite—surged to the presidency.
paragraphs.zh: 改革派让本次选举失手了，我就是来见证这场失利的。反对派一共推举了三位候选人，许多思想开明的伊朗人拒绝了他们三位，理由是改革路线已然失败，而伊朗的选举离自由还很远。于是，民粹主义的强硬派人物马哈茂德·艾哈迈迪内贾德——哈梅内伊最宠信的人——一路飙升，最终当选总统。

paragraphs.en: The morning the results came in, the red Peugeot was abnormally somber, Bahar and Niki absorbed in a nearly wordless grief. We were on our way to Tarbiat Modares University to see Hashem Aghajari, a reformist intellectual with a revolutionary background and a wooden leg that had replaced the one he’d lost in the Iran-Iraq War. Aghajari had been sentenced to death for a speech he gave in which he said that Muslims need not blindly follow a supreme leader, as though with “shackles around the neck.” Under popular pressure, including an international campaign to nominate him for the Nobel Peace Prize, the regime had commuted his sentence, but he still lived under the sword of Damocles, and I asked him whether the election results made him fearful.
paragraphs.zh: 结果公布的那天早上，红色的标致轿车异常沉闷，巴哈尔和尼基沉浸在几乎无言的悲伤中。我们正要去塔尔比亚特·莫达雷斯大学见哈西姆·阿加加里，一位有革命背景、并且用木腿替代了在两伊战争中失去的一条腿的改革派知识分子。阿加加里因一次演讲被判处死刑，演讲中他说穆斯林不必像用“镣铐锁住脖子”那样盲目服从至高领袖。在民间压力下，包括一场号召他获得诺贝尔和平奖的国际运动，政权改判了他的刑期，但他仍旧生活在达摩克利斯之剑下，我于是问他这次选举结果是否让他感到害怕。

paragraphs.en: “We have a saying in Farsi,” Aghajari replied. “ ‘There’s no shade darker than black.’ The worst they can do is execute me. I have prepared myself for that. If I am worried, it is not for myself. It’s for the Iranian people, for young people, today’s generation and future generations. My freedom and my life, and those of one or two people like me, don’t matter. They may take me to prison. I’m ready for that. In this society, we have no freedom to speak or to write. This is a prison, too.”
paragraphs.zh: “我们波斯语里有一句话，”阿加加里说，“‘没有比黑色更深的阴影。’他们能对我做的最坏的事就是处决我。为此我早有准备。如果我害怕，那不是为了我自己，而是为了伊朗人民、今天的年轻人，以及后代青年。我的自由和我的生命，和我这样的一两个人的生命，都不重要。他们可以把我送进监狱，我也准备好了。这个社会里，我们没有言论和写作的自由。这也是一座监狱。”

paragraphs.en: Outside Aghajari’s office, Bahar, or maybe Niki, motioned for us to sit a moment on a low brick wall in the university courtyard, where the sun beat down, and the two women wept.
paragraphs.zh: 在阿加贾里办公室外，巴哈尔，也许是妮基，让我们在大学院里的低矮砖墙上坐了一会儿，烈日当头，两个女人都哭了起来。

paragraphs.en: “When we have people like this in our country,” Bahar said at last, “why must we have Ahmadinejad as our president?”
paragraphs.zh: “既然我们国家里有了这样的人，”巴哈尔最后说，“我们为什么还要把哈米内伊当成总统？”

paragraphs.en: The author in Tehran in 2005 (Abbas / Magnum)
paragraphs.zh: “2005年在德黑兰的作者”（阿巴斯／马格南）

paragraphs.en: Reform was a conundrum like so many others under the Islamic Republic. It demanded cooperation and resistance at the same time—“pressure from below, negotiation at the top,” as one of its theorists articulated the strategy. The trouble was that Khamenei never once indicated that he would negotiate.
paragraphs.zh: 在伊斯兰共和国，改革也是众多难题之一。它要求在同一时间既要合作又要反抗——正如其理论家之一所概括的策略那样，“自下而上的压力、向上层施压的谈判”。问题在于哈梅内伊一次也没有表示他会谈判。

paragraphs.en: A compromise, practically by definition, satisfies no one. Reform was a compromise between hope and resignation. Iranian oppositionists grumbled about the movement’s timidity and its roots in the regime. The alternative, however, was confrontation, and throughout the period of my visits, Iranians were leery of it. The regime’s appetite and capacity for violence were never in doubt, and the country’s last revolution had gone very wrong. The movement behind it was broad-based, including liberals and leftists, but it was the Islamists who had emerged victorious in street battles and in politics, and who sealed their triumph through summary executions and imposed a theocratic state. This was not a distant memory. Mohsen Kadivar, a dissident cleric, once complained to me that his students railed against reform but shied away from rebellion. “If you won’t be the men of revolution,” Kadivar told me he said to them, “then be the boys of reform.”
paragraphs.zh: 妥协在定义上几乎无法让任何人满意。改革是希望与屈服之间的折中。伊朗反对派抱怨运动过于温和，而且其根基也在体制内。可替代方案却是对抗，而在我多次访问期间，伊朗人对对抗都很谨慎。政权对暴力的嗜好和动员能力从未让人怀疑，且国家上一次革命走得一塌糊涂。那场运动曾经范围广泛，既有自由主义者也有左翼人士，但在街头斗争和政治中胜出的却是伊斯兰主义者；他们用即决处决封住了胜局，建立了神权国家。那并不是遥远的记忆。异议神职人员穆罕默德·卡迪瓦（Mohsen Kadivar）曾对我抱怨过，他的学生们对改革口头上很不满，却总是避开叛乱。他对学生们说：“如果你们不想成为革命者，那就成为改革者。”

paragraphs.en: The last great showing of this meliorist current was the Green Movement of 2009. In that year’s presidential election, liberal-minded Iranians, including many who’d boycotted the 2005 vote, turned out in electrifying force for the moderate reformist candidates Mirhossein Mousavi and Mehdi Karroubi. On election day, some polls had barely closed when the regime called an implausible win for Ahmadinejad. Iranians I spoke with were incandescent with fury. Millions poured into the streets, and Mousavi and Karroubi eventually joined them there. The protesters didn’t demand an end to the Islamic Republic, even though many of them undoubtedly wished for it. They followed the cautious, legalistic reformist playbook and simply demanded that the system adhere to its own rules and allow them to elect the relatively moderate insider they’d voted for. They stood silently and held placards that read WHERE IS MY VOTE?
paragraphs.zh: 这股改良主义力量最后一次最盛大的亮相，是2009年的“绿色运动”。在那年总统选举中，不少原本在2005年投票中弃权的自由派伊朗人，转而以震撼性的规模走向投票站，支持中间派改革候选人米洛辛·穆萨维（Mirhossein Mousavi）和迈赫迪·卡鲁比（Mehdi Karroubi）。在选举日，当天一些民意调查还未“冷却”时，政权就宣布法齐勒·艾哈迈迪内贾德（Ahmadinejad）取得胜利，这一结果令人难以置信。与我交谈的伊朗人都愤怒得几乎爆发。数百万人涌上街头，穆萨维和卡鲁比最终也加入了人群。抗议者并没有要求结束伊斯兰共和国，尽管他们中许多人无疑是这么愿望的。他们遵循谨慎、合法主义的改革路线，只是要求政权执行自己的规则，并允许他们选出自己投票支持的相对温和的内部候选人。人群静静站立，举着“我的选票呢？”这样的标语牌。

paragraphs.en: Bahar called me in New York on the day that the crowd was at its maximum in Tehran’s Azadi Square. She was enraptured; the atmosphere was like nothing she had ever known. The barriers of suspicion, private humiliation, and pain that had divided people for decades seemed to drop away in that expanse of shared silence, and the sense of common purpose was like a current passing through the crowd. To her special delight, she saw Aghajari not far from her—maneuvering, unafraid, on his wooden leg.
paragraphs.zh: 贝哈尔在纽约给我打来电话，那时德黑兰阿扎迪广场的人群达到了顶峰。她被迷住了；那种氛围是她从未经历过的。长期以来因猜疑、个人羞辱和伤痛而把人们隔开的壁垒，似乎在这片共享沉默的空间中消融了，集体目标的感觉像一股电流穿过人群。令她格外欣喜的是，她在不远处看见了阿加哈吉里（Aghajari），他一边用木腿灵活挪动，一边毫不畏惧。

paragraphs.en: The Green Movement was the largest, most sustained, and most organized campaign of street protests that the Islamic Republic ever confronted. Foreign commenters sometimes mistook this for the spontaneous cri de coeur of a thwarted presidential campaign, mobilized by Twitter posts, but in fact it was a movement with a history, layers of experienced leaders, painstakingly articulated ideas, a pragmatic strategy, and a networked constituency. Precisely for this reason, the Islamic Republic set about destroying it with bullets, tear gas, batons, and torture.
paragraphs.zh: 绿色运动是伊斯兰共和国曾面对过的、规模最大、持续时间最长、组织最有序的一次街头抗议运动。外国评论者有时误把它当作被扼杀的总统竞选发出的即兴呐喊，以为由推特帖子所推动，但实际上它是一场有历史、有经验层层沉淀领导人的运动，有经过反复梳理的思想体系、务实的策略，以及有组织的选民网络。正因为如此，伊斯兰共和国才着手用子弹、催泪瓦斯、警棍和酷刑将其彻底摧毁。

paragraphs.en: Ahmadinejad’s first term had already seen the closure of virtually all of the reformist publications and NGOs and the exclusion of reformist candidates from campaigns for most public offices. Now the regime arrested enough of the movement’s leaders and activists to fill an auditorium, where they were paraded, hollow-eyed, in prison pajamas and forced to confess to outlandish conspiracies. Lesser-known young activists were remanded to a fetid metal shipping container in the desert. Many were raped and killed. By 2010, even to speak of or publish a photo of former President Khatami was forbidden; the cautious reform movement was dubbed “the sedition,” and Mousavi and Karroubi were placed under a draconian house arrest that would endure for a decade and a half.
paragraphs.zh: 阿哈迈迪内贾德的第一任期已经见证了几乎所有改革派出版物和非政府组织被关闭，改革派候选人几乎被排除在大多数公职竞选之外。现在，政权逮捕了足够多的该运动领导人和活动人士，足以坐满一个礼堂，让他们被押到那里，面色惨白地穿着监狱服，在众目睽睽之下“出场”，并被迫承认一些荒谬的阴谋。许多不太出名的年轻活动人士被拘押在沙漠里的一个恶臭金属货运集装箱里。许多人遭到强奸并被杀害。到2010年，仅仅提及或公开前总统卡塔米的照片都被禁止；谨慎的改革运动被称为“煽动叛乱”，而穆萨维和卡鲁比被置于严苛的软禁之下，持续了整整十五年。

paragraphs.en: When the Arab Spring came to Tunisia, Egypt, and other countries not long after the Green Movement was crushed, I ached for Iran. Of all the countries in the Middle East, up until 2009, Iran had perhaps the most credible infrastructure for democratic change—and one of the most obdurate autocracies.
paragraphs.zh: 在“绿色运动”遭到镇压后不久，阿拉伯之春波及突尼斯、埃及等国时，我为伊朗深感痛心。到2009年前，在中东所有国家中，伊朗也许拥有最有可信度的民主转型基础设施，却也是最顽固、最难缠的专制政体之一。

paragraphs.en: My fifth and last visit to Iran, in 2012, felt in many ways like a bookend to the first, but with the coercion unmasked. Foreign journalists had been mostly excluded from the country since 2009, but three years later, I was part of a small group permitted to observe a parliamentary election. We were marched onto buses and driven to photo ops not of our choosing; even the top bureaucrats assigned to corral us made rueful jokes rather than pretend, per usual, that any of this was for our safety. Talking with a Green Movement activist required an assignation in a moving car after dark. Once again, I found myself studying the apparatus that stood in the way of studying anything else.
paragraphs.zh: 我2012年的第五次也是最后一次访伊朗，在许多方面像是和第一次一样，像是一道书页的另一端，但这次是看清了胁迫的真面目。自2009年以来，外国记者大多被排除在伊朗之外，但三年后，我却能作为一个小团体的一员进入国会选举的观察行列。我们被带上巴士，被驱车前往非我们选择的“拍照场景”；甚至负责管理我们的一线官员也不再假装平常那样说这是为了我们安全，而是苦笑着开些讽刺的玩笑。与一位“绿色运动”活动人士交谈，需要在傍晚后于行驶中的汽车里秘密会见。又一次，我发现自己在研究着这套阻碍一切其他观察的机器。

paragraphs.en: Just hours before I left the country, agents from the IRGC apprehended me for questioning because I had left my hotel at night without a minder. They interrogated me about my movements, my contacts, the time I asked my minder to take me to a butcher to confirm popular complaints about the price of chicken. “You have not behaved,” an interrogator told me—and, more ominously: “We think you’re not a journalist. We think you’re a spy.”
paragraphs.zh: 就在我离境的几个小时前，伊斯兰革命卫队的特工因为我深夜离开酒店时没有带看护人而把我带走盘问。审问中，他们问我在外的行踪、我的联络对象，还有我何时要求看护人带我去屠宰场核实民众对鸡价的抱怨。审讯官对我说：“你没有按规矩办事。”更不祥的是，他又补了一句：“我们认为你不是记者。我们认为你是间谍。”

paragraphs.en: If they had actually believed this, they might have detained me indefinitely. But in the end I think they meant only to intimidate me. In the third hour of our interview, the interrogator seized my belongings and left the room with them. He returned in a fury and threw a folder of mine on his desk.
paragraphs.zh: 如果他们真信了这件事，可能会把我无限期扣留。后来我想，他们大概只是想恐吓我。我们谈话到第三个小时，盘问者拿走了我的随身物品并带出房间。接着他怒气冲冲地回来，把我一个文件夹重重扔到桌上。

paragraphs.en: “Do you think we are not intelligent?” he demanded. “We’re keeping your receipts.” He waved in front of me the ones I’d collected for reimbursement for my travel expenses on my return. (I later realized that he may have thought I’d kept them to document the inflation that the government was at that moment trying to conceal from its citizens.) “And we are keeping this.” He held up one of the two extra passport-size photos I’d had taken for my visa. But I was a journalist, he conceded, and he let me go.
paragraphs.zh: “你们以为我们不聪明吗？”他咆哮道。“我们已经扣押了你的收据。”他向我晃了晃我回国后报销差旅费时留下的那些单据。（后来我才意识到，他可能以为我保存这些是为了记录通货膨胀，而政府当时正试图对公民隐瞒这一点。）“这张也会被扣下。”他举起我为办理签证拍的两张护照照片中的一张。但他也承认我是一名记者，于是就放我走了。

paragraphs.en: By that time, Iran’s democratic infrastructure had mostly been torched. But the yearning and anger it had once harnessed only grew and became more confrontational. At times, opposition still attached itself to elections—to whoever among the allowable candidates represented the most liberal edge of the possible. But it also exploded in street protests of a qualitatively new kind, such as those that erupted in 2017 and early 2018, when members of the lower classes in provincial cities openly reviled the Islamic Republic and chanted “Death to Khamenei.” For another hot minute, the world held its breath for the Islamic Republic to collapse. Instead, it killed.
paragraphs.zh: 到那时，伊朗的民主基础设施大多已被焚毁。但其曾经汇聚的渴望与愤怒只会更加强烈，也更具对抗性。有时反对派仍旧依附于选举——支持在允许参选者中最偏自由的一端；但它也以一种性质全新的方式在街头爆发，例如2017年和2018年初出现的抗议，当时许多省会城市的底层群众公开痛骂伊斯兰共和国，并高呼“卡梅内伊死刑”。又有那么一阵，世界都屏住呼吸，等待伊斯兰共和国的崩塌；结果，它却选择了杀戮。

paragraphs.en: To imagine that this cycle would repeat itself in 2022 was almost unbearable. Outraged by the death of a young woman in the custody of the morality police, women and teenage girls made hijab the symbolic center of their revolt. The headscarf was both a tool and a symbol of suffocation: Removing it publicly, en masse, was an act of civil disobedience without precedent under the Islamic Republic. Although the “Women, Life, Freedom” uprising was violently quelled like all the rest—some 500 dead, maybe 20,000 in prison, families forbidden even to publicly mourn—it left a uniquely durable legacy, in that women began appearing uncovered in public with relative impunity. This was something new and promising. But it did not bring democracy or suggest what could.
paragraphs.zh: 设想这轮循环会在2022年重演，几乎令人无法承受。年轻女子在道德警察羁押中死亡的事件激起了义愤，女性和少女把头巾（希贾布）推到了反抗的象征中心。头巾既是压迫工具，也是束缚的象征：在伊斯兰共和国体制下公开、成群地脱下头巾，是前所未有的公民不服从行为。尽管“女性，生命，自由”式的起义像以往每一次那样被血腥镇压——约500人遇害，约两万人被关进监狱，家属甚至不许公开哀悼——它却留下了独特而持久的后果：越来越多女性开始在公共场合露出面部，且相对不用担心被立即惩处。这是新的、令人振奋的变化，但它既没有带来民主，也没有给民主的形态与前景定下答案。

paragraphs.en: Iran was and remains a heartbreaker. Where else is the civic spirit so enduring, and so unyieldingly denied? Time and again, the Islamic Republic proved itself implacable before even the most rudimentary of its subjects’ desires. It valued neither their lives nor any legitimacy that their consent could confer on the state. It refused them the dignity of small freedoms that might have cost the system nothing. And it would not even afford them a stake in prosperity: Over the course of the first two decades of the 21st century, a largely middle-class country was driven to penury, not only because of international sanctions, but because of the voracious corruption of the IRGC, which Khamenei allowed and encouraged as a means of hoarding power.
paragraphs.zh: 伊朗曾经如此，如今也仍然让人心碎。还有哪个国家能有如此顽强的公民精神，却被如此不屈不挠地剥夺？一再证明的是，伊斯兰共和国在面对民众最最基本的愿望时都显得铁石心肠。它既不在乎他们的生命，也不在乎人民同意所能赋予政权的任何合法性。它拒绝给予那些几乎不需要付出代价的小小自由尊严。更不愿让他们分得一杯成功果实：在21世纪的前二十年里，伊朗这个以中产阶级为主体的国家被推向了贫困，不仅是因为国际制裁，还因为伊斯兰革命卫队（IRGC）贪婪的腐败，而这一切都在哈梅内伊的允许和鼓励下，以巩固权力为名得以延续。

paragraphs.en: I’ll admit that I distanced myself from Iran. My run-in with the IRGC had made traveling there again impossible, and I questioned the value of what I could observe from afar. My network of sources outside the country had always been eclectic. Now it spanned a venomously polarized diaspora that traded accusations—of complicity with Iran’s foreign enemies, and with the ever more hated regime.
paragraphs.zh: 我承认自己曾与伊朗保持距离。与IRGC的冲突让我不可能再去那里，而我也开始怀疑，隔着距离我到底能看到多少。此前我在国内之外建立的消息网络本就很杂。现在它蔓延到一个彼此极端对立的侨民社群，在那里，指控来来往往——指责彼此与伊朗的外国敌人勾结，也指责彼此与越来越被憎恨的政权合作。

paragraphs.en: To hope for change in Iran was quixotic; to bet against it seemed cruel. Each upswell of protest presented a breathtaking display of youthful courage shadowed by near-certain tragedy. Eternally optimistic, a friend inside Iran offered me a metaphor: If it takes 100 blows of the axe to cut down a tree, he wrote to me on WhatsApp in 2022, you don’t say the first 99 were useless.
paragraphs.zh: 要对伊朗的变革抱有希望，本身就有些痴人说梦；而在这场希望中下注反对它似乎又过于残酷。每一次浪潮般的抗议，都呈现出惊人的青春勇气，但又都笼罩在几乎注定悲剧的阴影之下。一个一贯乐观的伊朗朋友在2022年给我发了条WhatsApp信息：如果砍倒一棵树需要一百下斧砍，你不能因为前99下就说它们全是白费。

paragraphs.en: But the Islamic Republic seemed to be made of ironwood. It was not one man’s dictatorship. The revolutionaries had built institutions, both civilian and military, that perpetuated themselves. Networks of violence ran deep, through virtually every power center and organ of the system, and the regime retained a considerable base of ideological support in both the populace and the security apparatus. Time and again, asked to choose between their neighbors and their leaders, Iran’s men under arms chose the regime.
paragraphs.zh: 但伊斯兰共和国似乎是由坚韧的铁木构成的。它并非一人专政。革命者建立了既有民事也有军事实体的各种制度，并让这些制度自我延续。贯穿几乎所有权力中心与制度器官的暴力网络触角深入；政权在民众和安全系统中都保有可观的意识形态基础。一次又一次，当不得不在邻居与领袖之间作出选择时，拿起武器的伊朗男性往往站在了政权一边。

paragraphs.en: Would they really do it? Would they open fire on unarmed crowds of mostly young people, mowing them down by the thousands? This past January, the Islamic Republic made its security forces the instrument of an atrocity of world-historic proportions, killing at least 6,000 and possibly more than 30,000 protesters. Something broke inside of nearly every Iranian I knew. Or ignited: a fireball of rage and trauma. How could one live under such a regime? But what form of resistance was possible? One exiled activist told me privately that she fantasized about returning as an armed resistance fighter: “The reality is that we have reached a point, a dead end, where you almost have to be a partisan to win. Otherwise you have to accept that they will kill you and move on.”
paragraphs.zh: 他们真的会那样做吗？他们会不会对几乎全是年轻人的不持武器人群开枪，把他们一层层扫射到千百万人？在过去的1月，伊斯兰共和国把安全部队变成了世界历史上空前规模的暴行工具，至少杀害了6,000人，可能更多，超过30,000名抗议者。几乎每一个我认识的伊朗人心里都被某种东西打碎了，或者被点燃：一团由愤怒与创伤组成的火球。一个人在这样的体制下还能怎样生存？但又有哪种反抗才是可能的？一位流亡活动家私下告诉我，她幻想过以武装反抗者的身份归来：“现实是，我们已经走到了一个节点、一个死胡同，几乎不得不站到一边打仗才有机会赢。否则你只能接受他们会杀了你并继续前进。”

paragraphs.en: When American and Israeli bombs began blasting their homeland, many of my old friends and contacts hitched their country’s epic hopes to Trump’s epic fury. No nonviolent effort had dislodged or even shaken the regime; here at last was hard power. The alternative was the Islamic Republic, forever. But others among my old network were aghast. The hard power in question was wielded by outsiders for who knew what purpose, against a continuously widening ambit of targets. One friend texted me to ask: If the Iranians celebrating such violent destruction of their country come to power by means of it, can they really be said to be pro-democratic? How will they treat their opponents?
paragraphs.zh: 当美以色列的轰炸开始袭击故土时，我很多老朋友和熟人都把他们对国家的远大希望系在特朗普那股近乎史诗般的愤怒上。非暴力的努力既没有把政权赶走，甚至都没真正动摇它；而眼前终于出现了硬实力。另一路径就是伊斯兰共和国、永无止境地继续存在。可是，我旧有网络中的其他人却惊骇不已。所谓的“硬实力”是由局外人出于谁也说不清的目的挥舞着，朝着不断扩大的目标群体发动打击。一位朋友发短信来问我：如果那群在这种暴力摧毁中为自己的国家庆祝的人，最后正是凭借这种方式登上权力高位，那么他们真的能被称作亲民主吗？他们会怎样对待自己的反对者？

paragraphs.en: Lately I’ve been wondering whether a fault line has always run through the opposition, or if what I’m seeing now is new. On one side are those who still believe that despite the outcome of the 1979 revolution, its broadest animating impulses—the rejection of monarchy and American dominion, and the assertion of Iran’s sovereignty over its resources and political fate—are sacrosanct. On the other are those who have concluded that not only the Islamic Republic, but the revolution itself, was a wrong turn. There is a potent symbolism in their embrace of the son of the deposed shah as a leader for the future, and their acceptance of American force as the means for empowering him.
paragraphs.zh: 最近我一直在想：反对阵营里是否一直都存在一道裂缝，抑或我现在看到的是一种新现象。坐在一边的是那些尽管1979年革命的结果很复杂，却仍然相信其最核心驱动力——反对君主制和美国支配，以及维护伊朗对其资源和命运主权——是神圣不可侵犯的人。另一边则是那些已经得出结论的人：不仅伊斯兰共和国本身，连那场革命本身都走错了方向。对被罢黜国王的儿子作为未来领导者的拥抱，和将美国武力视作赋予他权力的手段，这两件事都具有强烈的象征意味。

paragraphs.en: The views of these camps are anathema to each other. I am trying to listen respectfully to both—though truth be told, I cannot at the time of this writing imagine a way that this war ends in Iranian liberation, or a way that the Islamic Republic, with or without the war, decides to yield. But how can I say these things, or even think them? Not when every phone call ends with a promise—that we’ll continue the conversation, someday, in Tehran.
paragraphs.zh: 这两个阵营的观点彼此完全对立。此刻我正努力对双方都保持尊重倾听——说实话，目前写下这些时，我想象不出这场战争如何会以伊朗解放收场，也想象不出伊斯兰共和国在有无战争的情况下会怎样选择让步。但我如何说出这些，或者哪怕在心里想出这些？当每一次通话都以一个承诺结束：‘总有一天我们会在德黑兰继续谈这件事。’

paragraphs.en: This article appears in the May 2026 print edition with the headline “Someday in Tehran.”
paragraphs.zh: 这篇文章出现在2026年5月的纸媒版面，标题为“Someday in Tehran”。
